body: We need to add eslint rule, that will detect comments, and strings in the code, that looks like comments about what changed and fail the check.

comments.body:
## Proposal: ESLint rule `no-changelog-comments` (default: `warn`)

Both AI agents and humans tend to turn code into a changelog — they write *what
changed* instead of *what is*. Comments like "previously X, now Y", "removed the
old guard", "after the fix", plus dates and PR/issue numbers, accumulate inside
the source. That history belongs in git, PRs and docs — not in the code, which
readers consume as the **current** state of the system.

A lint rule catches most of these automatically. Suggested shape below.

### Severity

Default to **`warn`**, not `error`. The signal is heuristic (keyword/structure
based) and will have false positives, so it should nudge, not block CI by
default. Projects can opt into `error` explicitly.

### What it inspects

- line and block comments (`//`, `/* */`, JSDoc);
- string literals and template literals (user-facing log / CLI / TUI text tends
  to carry the same changelog phrasing).

### Detection: keywords (domain-free, multilingual)

Only generic "history" connectives — **no domain vocabulary**. Match
case-insensitively, on word boundaries. Cover at least English, Russian, Chinese,
Hindi:

- **English:** `previously`, `formerly`, `used to`, `no longer`, `renamed`,
  `moved out`, `after the fix`, `as before`, `back-compat` / `backwards compat`,
  and `now` / `before` **only when paired** with a change verb (see structure
  below — bare `now`/`before` are too common to flag alone).
- **Russian:** `раньше`, `ранее`, `прежде`, `прежн(ий|яя|ее)`, `теперь`,
  `больше не`, `после правки`, `после перехода`, `переименован`, `вынесен(о)`,
  `перенесён`, `как раньше`, `по-прежнему`.
- **Chinese:** `以前`, `之前`, `原来`, `现在改为`, `不再`, `重命名`, `已移除`,
  `曾经`, `改成了`.
- **Hindi:** `पहले`, `पहले था`, `अब नहीं`, `बदल दिया`, `हटा दिया`, `नाम बदला`,
  `पहले की तरह`।

### Detection: regular expressions (the change *structure*, not just words)

Keywords alone miss phrasing and over-match common words. Pairing a
**history-marker** with a **change/state verb or an old↔new contrast** is the
stronger signal. Suggested patterns (validated under Node, `iu`/`u` flags):

> ⚠️ Word-boundary gotcha: `\b` only works around ASCII/Latin. **Do not** wrap
> Cyrillic / Devanagari / CJK markers in `\b` — `/\bраньше\b/u` never matches.
> Use `\b` for English only; for other scripts match the marker directly (and, if
> needed, gate it on a nearby verb instead of a boundary). `\w` itself *does* match
> Cyrillic under `u`, so `был\w*` is fine — it's `\b` that fails.

```js
const CHANGELOG_PATTERNS = [
  // EN — marker paired with a state/change verb: "previously it was X", "used to return Y"
  /\b(?:previously|formerly|used to|no longer|once|originally)\b[\s\S]{0,40}?\b(?:was|were|is|are|did|do|does|had|has|have|used|use|uses|call(?:s|ed)?|name[ds]?|return(?:s|ed)?|live[ds]?|work(?:s|ed)?)\b/iu,
  // EN — old→new contrast
  /\b(?:instead of|rather than)\b/iu,
  /\b(?:changed|renamed|moved|switched|migrated|replaced)\b[\s\S]{0,30}?\b(?:from|to)\b/iu,
  // EN — "now" as a change marker (paired with a verb + contrast), not a bare timing word
  /\bnow\b[\s\S]{0,30}?\b(?:uses?|does|is|are|returns?|lives?|points?|goes?)\b[\s\S]{0,30}?\b(?:instead|no longer|rather)\b/iu,

  // RU — no \b (fails on Cyrillic); marker + past/state verb, or a standalone change marker
  /(?:раньше|ранее|прежде|прежн\w+|до этого|до фикса)[\s\S]{0,40}?(?:был\w*|делал\w*|использовал\w*|называл\w*|возвращал\w*|жил\w*)/iu,
  /(?:теперь|больше не|сменил\w*|перешл\w*|вынес\w*|перенес\w*|переименова\w*)/iu,

  // ZH — contrast / removal / rename markers
  /(?:以前|之前|原来|曾经)[\s\S]{0,20}?(?:是|用|叫|返回|改)/u,
  /(?:现在改为|改成了?|不再|已移除|已重命名)/u,

  // HI — history markers
  /(?:पहले|पहले था|अब नहीं|बदल दिया|हटा दिया|नाम बदला)/u,

  // Cross-language — explicit history refs: dates and PR/issue numbers in a comment/string
  /\b(?:PR|issue|pull request)\s*#\d+/iu,
  /\b\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}\b/u,                  // ISO date inside a comment/string
];
```

### Important: exclude non-changelog look-alikes (avoid false positives)

These read like markers but describe **the present**, so the rule should NOT
flag them (or projects should be able to allowlist them):

- **execution order**, not history: "return early", "process longer items first",
  "after we start the server, not before".
- **state at a step**: "no longer needed *at this point*", "the lock is already
  released here".
- **references to external/upstream bug trackers** as a pointer to a known
  third-party defect (e.g. an upstream `#39753`) — that is not *your* changelog.

A practical heuristic: a bare timing word (`now`, `before`, `после`) is fine; it
only becomes a changelog smell when paired with a **change verb or an old↔new
contrast**, which is why the regexes above pair the two rather than matching the
keyword alone.

### Suggested rule options

```jsonc
{
  "no-changelog-comments": ["warn", {
    "languages": ["en", "ru", "zh", "hi"],   // which keyword sets to enable
    "checkStrings": true,                      // also scan string/template literals
    "allow": ["#39753"],                       // allowlist (e.g. upstream tracker refs)
    "allowDatesInStrings": false               // dates inside user-facing strings
  }]
}
```

### Why this helps

The goal is a fast, automated nudge: *describe how the code works now and why, not
how it changed.* As a `warn`-level rule it keeps CI green while steadily training
both humans and AI agents away from baking history into source.

We need to collect data related about the issue to this repository, make sure we compile that data to `./docs/case-studies/issue-{id}` folder, and use it to do deep case study analysis (also make sure to search online for additional facts and data), list of each and all requirements from the issue, and propose possible solutions and solution plans for each requirement (we should also check known existing components/libraries, that solve similar problem or can help in solutions).

Please plan and execute everything in this single pull request, you have unlimited time and context, as context auto-compacts and you can continue indefinitely, until it is each and every requirement fully addressed, and everything is totally done.

title: Code is not a change log
